title: How to Set Up a Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA) for an S-Corp

interlinking_suggestions.title: Canada's Digital Nomad Stream: How to Live and Work in Canada

author.name: Ben Carter
author.bio: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: As an S-Corp owner with over 2% equity, you are legally ineligible to participate in a company Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA); attempting to do so will result in taxable income.

To make your personal health insurance tax-deductible, ensure your S-Corp formally establishes and pays the premiums, reports them as income on your W-2, and you then take the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction on your personal tax return.
Leverage an HRA (like a QSEHRA or ICHRA) as a strategic tool to offer tax-free health benefits to your non-owner employees, gaining a competitive hiring advantage with full budget control.
Verify that your payroll provider includes your health premiums in Box 1 of your W-2 but excludes them from Social Security and Medicare wages in Boxes 3 and 5 to ensure correct tax treatment.
Recognize that IRS family attribution rules extend HRA ineligibility to your spouse, children, and parents, requiring them to follow the same owner-benefit process if they are employees.
summary: S-Corp owners with over a 2% stake face a common problem: they are personally ineligible for tax-free Health Reimbursement Arrangements (HRAs), creating compliance risks. The core advice is a two-part strategy: owners should secure their own benefits by having the S-Corp pay their premiums and then take the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction, while separately using an HRA as a powerful tool to offer competitive, tax-free benefits to non-owner employees. By adopting this dual approach, owners can transform a regulatory limitation into a strategic advantage, optimizing their personal tax situation while building a more competitive business.
ai_overview_answer: No, an S-Corp owner with more than a 2% ownership stake is personally ineligible to participate in a Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA), including a QSEHRA or ICHRA. The IRS classifies these owners as self-employed for fringe benefit purposes, making them ineligible for an employee-only benefit like an HRA. However, owners can still receive tax-advantaged health benefits through a specific, IRS-compliant method. The S-Corp must formally establish and pay for the owner's health insurance policy, deducting the premiums as officer compensation. These premium payments must then be reported as income on the owner's Form W-2. This allows the owner to take the 100% Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction on their personal tax return, which effectively makes the benefit tax-free. While the owner cannot use an HRA, the S-Corporation can offer one to its non-owner W-2 employees as a powerful tool to attract and retain talent.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

@graph.headline: How to Set Up a Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA) for an S-Corp
@graph.description: Confused about an HRA for S-Corp owners? Get the definitive IRS rule. Learn the correct strategy to secure tax-free health benefits for you & your family.

step.name: The Business Establishes and Pays for the Plan.
step.text: This is the most critical and often missed step. Your S-Corporation must formally establish the health insurance policy. This means the business must either pay the premiums directly to the insurance provider or reimburse you for premiums you paid personally. This creates the necessary legal foundation for the deduction. A personal plan you simply ask the business to pay for, without this formal structure, will not qualify.

step.name: The Business Deducts Premiums as Compensation.
step.text: By paying the premiums, the S-Corporation can deduct the full amount as officer compensation on its tax return (Form 1120-S), reducing the company's taxable profit. This correctly categorizes the expense as part of your total compensation package.

step.name: Report Premiums as W-2 Income.
step.text: This is the mandatory administrative step that unlocks your personal deduction. The total amount of the premiums paid by the S-Corp must be included as income on your Form W-2, specifically in Box 1 (Wages). While this increases your gross income on paper, these amounts are generally not subject to Social Security or Medicare (FICA) taxes and should not appear in Box 3 or 5.

step.name: Take the 100% Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction.
step.text: Now, the strategy comes full circle. On your personal tax return (Form 1040), you take the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction. This is a powerful "above-the-line" deduction, meaning it directly reduces your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI). The result is that you get the full deduction whether you itemize or take the standard deduction, effectively making the health benefit tax-free by offsetting the income added in Step 3.

mainEntity.name: Can my S-Corp reimburse me for a health insurance plan from the marketplace?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, absolutely. The source of the policy does not matter, but the process does. Your S-Corp can reimburse you for a marketplace plan as long as the plan is formally established by the business, the company pays for it (or reimburses you), and the total premium cost is included in your W-2 wages. You then take the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction on your personal return.

mainEntity.name: Does the HRA ineligibility rule apply to my family members?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. The IRS uses "family attribution" rules to determine ownership. For benefit purposes, an individual is considered to own the stock held by their spouse, children, grandchildren, and parents. Therefore, if their combined ownership with yours exceeds 2%, these family members are also ineligible to participate in a company HRA, even if they are W-2 employees. They must use the same "Owner's Playbook" strategy to receive tax-advantaged health benefits.

mainEntity.name: Is an HRA a good benefit for my non-owner employees?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, it is an exceptional strategic tool. An HRA (like a QSEHRA or ICHRA) provides a competitive edge in the talent market by allowing you to offer a significant, tax-free health benefit with complete budgetary control. It empowers your employees with the choice to select an insurance plan that best fits their needs, a feature highly valued in the modern workforce.

mainEntity.name: What makes the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction so powerful?

acceptedAnswer.text: Its power lies in being an "above-the-line" deduction. This means it directly reduces your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI), which can have a cascading positive effect on other aspects of your tax liability. Unlike a standard medical expense deduction, you get the full benefit whether you itemize or take the standard deduction, and you don't need to meet a high spending threshold to qualify.

body.content: As the CEO of your "Business-of-One," you navigate complex decisions daily. But when it comes to your own health benefits, the rules for S-Corporations can feel less like a strategic choice and more like a compliance trap. You have likely heard about the power of a Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA), a popular tool for providing tax-free benefits, only to be met with a confusing wall of IRS regulations that seem to exclude you. This ambiguity is frustrating and creates risk—the last thing you need while building your business.

This guide moves you from uncertainty to command. We will reframe this challenge by showing you that the regulations are not a barrier, but a signpost pointing toward a specific and powerful path. To achieve this, we will break down the strategy into two clear, actionable playbooks:

items: **The Owner's Playbook:** A step-by-step guide to maximizing your personal, tax-advantaged health benefits in full compliance with IRS rules.
**The CEO's Playbook:** A plan for leveraging an HRA to build a competitive business that attracts and retains top talent.

body.content: This is your guide to turning a source of compliance anxiety into a strategic advantage for both your personal wealth and your company's growth.
The Foundational Rule: Can an S-Corp Owner Participate in an HRA?
Before building your strategy, we must establish the single most important rule that governs this landscape. Let’s cut through the noise and answer your core question directly.

The unequivocal answer is **no**. If you are an S-Corp owner with more than a 2% ownership stake, you are personally ineligible to participate in any type of Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA), including a QSEHRA or ICHRA. This isn't a loophole or a gray area; it is a clear line drawn by the IRS.

This rule is rooted in how the IRS classifies you. For the purposes of fringe benefits, the tax code treats S-Corp owners who hold more than a 2% stake as self-employed individuals, not as conventional W-2 employees. Since an HRA is specifically designed as an employee benefit, this classification makes you, the owner, ineligible by definition. Attempting to use an HRA for yourself would result in those reimbursements being treated as taxable income, defeating the entire purpose.

However, this is the critical distinction that transforms a limitation into an opportunity: while **you** cannot participate, your S-Corporation can absolutely offer a powerful HRA to your non-owner W-2 employees.

This "no" is not a dead end. It is a pivot. It directs you away from an employee-centric tool and toward a different, equally powerful strategy designed specifically for you. Understanding this foundational rule is the first step in taking control.
The Owner's Playbook: Securing Your Tax-Advantaged Health Benefits

The regulation preventing your HRA participation doesn't block you; it guides you to a powerful strategy designed specifically for S-Corp owners. This isn't a workaround—it's the intended, tax-efficient method for leveraging your business to pay for your personal health insurance. Executing this four-step playbook correctly is the key to transforming a personal expense into a significant business advantage.

items: **Step 1: The Business Establishes and Pays for the Plan.** This is the most critical and often missed step. Your S-Corporation must formally establish the health insurance policy. This means the business must either pay the premiums directly to the insurance provider or reimburse you for premiums you paid personally. This creates the necessary legal foundation for the deduction. A personal plan you simply ask the business to pay for, without this formal structure, will not qualify.

**Step 2: The Business Deducts Premiums as Compensation.** By paying the premiums, the S-Corporation can deduct the full amount as officer compensation on its tax return (Form 1120-S), reducing the company's taxable profit. This correctly categorizes the expense as part of your total compensation package.

**Step 3: Report Premiums as W-2 Income.** This is the mandatory administrative step that unlocks your personal deduction. The total amount of the premiums paid by the S-Corp must be included as income on your Form W-2, specifically in Box 1 (Wages). While this increases your gross income on paper, these amounts are generally not subject to Social Security or Medicare (FICA) taxes and should not appear in Box 3 or 5.

**Step 4: Take the 100% Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction.** Now, the strategy comes full circle. On your personal tax return (Form 1040), you take the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction. This is a powerful "**above-the-line**" deduction, meaning it directly reduces your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI). The result is that you get the full deduction whether you itemize or take the standard deduction, effectively making the health benefit tax-free by offsetting the income added in Step 3.

body.content: The CEO's Playbook: Using an HRA to Win and Retain Talent

With your personal health benefit strategy secured, you can shift your focus from owner to CEO. While you cannot personally use an HRA, your S-Corp can and should deploy one as a powerful tool to build your team. This is an investment in a strategic advantage that allows your firm to compete with—and win against—larger companies for top-tier talent.
Gain a Competitive Edge

In a competitive hiring market, a tax-free health benefit is a powerful differentiator. An HRA gives your S-Corp the leverage of a "big company" benefit without the administrative complexity and cost volatility of traditional group plans. You empower your team with choice and flexibility, transforming the benefits conversation from a simple cost item into a compelling part of your company's value proposition.
Choose Your HRA: QSEHRA vs. ICHRA

Your primary options are the Qualified Small Employer HRA (QSEHRA) and the Individual Coverage HRA (ICHRA). While both allow you to reimburse employees tax-free for their health insurance premiums and medical costs, they serve different strategic needs. The QSEHRA is built for simplicity, while the ICHRA offers maximum flexibility and scalability.

rows: No size limit; suitable for any business.
**$6,350** for an individual and **$12,800** for a family.
The allowance must be offered on the same terms to all eligible employees.
Highly flexible; you can create different allowance amounts for different employee classes (e.g., salaried vs. hourly, geographic location).
Cannot be offered alongside a group health plan.
Can be offered to certain classes of employees even if another class is offered a traditional group plan.

body.content: Maintain Absolute Budgetary Control

Traditional group health plans come with the dread of significant annual premium hikes. An HRA reverses this dynamic. You determine a fixed monthly allowance for your employees—and that is your maximum financial exposure. If employees don't use their full allowance, the funds remain with the company. This model provides absolute cost certainty, allowing you to budget precisely.
Empower Your Employees with Choice

Modern talent values autonomy. An HRA honors their individuality by empowering them to select the personal health insurance plan that is best for them and their families. They choose the network, the carrier, and the coverage levels that fit their lives. This act of trusting your employees with their own healthcare decisions is a powerful statement that fosters loyalty and positions your business as a modern, attractive place to work.
S-Corp Owner's Benefits: A Compliance Checklist

Executing the Owner's Playbook correctly is critical. Getting the details wrong can lead to lost deductions and unwanted IRS attention. Use this checklist to structure your benefits correctly and facilitate a productive conversation with your CPA.

items: **Formal Plan Establishment:** Ensure your health insurance plan is formally "established by the business." This means the S-Corporation either pays premiums directly or formally reimburses you. A corporate resolution in your minutes officially adopting the plan is a best practice to create a clear legal foundation.

**Meticulous Documentation:** Maintain an organized file with copies of insurance policy documents, proof of premium payments from the S-Corp's bank account (or proof of reimbursement), and payroll records explicitly showing the premiums treated as shareholder wages. This paper trail is your best defense in an audit.

**Proactive Payroll Coordination:** Do not assume your payroll provider will handle this correctly without explicit instruction. Proactively confirm that the total premium amount is included in **Box 1** of your Form W-2 and, crucially, *excluded* from wages for Social Security and Medicare (FICA) in **Boxes 3 and 5**. Verify this setup long before W-2s are finalized.

**Personal Tax Return Accuracy:** Double-check your completed tax return (Form 1040, Schedule 1) to confirm your preparer has taken the **Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction** and that the amount matches the total premiums reported on your W-2. Be your own best advocate.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
Navigating the nuances of S-Corp benefits can raise specific questions. This quick-reference guide provides direct answers to the most common sticking points.
**Can my S-Corp reimburse me for a health insurance plan from the marketplace?**

Yes, absolutely. The source of the policy does not matter, but the process does. Your S-Corp can reimburse you for a marketplace plan as long as the plan is formally established by the business, the company pays for it (or reimburses you), and the total premium cost is included in your W-2 wages. You then take the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction on your personal return.
**Does the HRA ineligibility rule apply to my family members?**

Yes. The IRS uses "family attribution" rules to determine ownership. For benefit purposes, an individual is considered to own the stock held by their spouse, children, grandchildren, and parents. Therefore, if their combined ownership with yours exceeds 2%, these family members are also ineligible to participate in a company HRA, even if they are W-2 employees. They must use the same "Owner's Playbook" strategy to receive tax-advantaged health benefits.
**Is an HRA a good benefit for my non-owner employees?**

Yes, it is an exceptional strategic tool. An HRA (like a QSEHRA or ICHRA) provides a competitive edge in the talent market by allowing you to offer a significant, tax-free health benefit with complete budgetary control. It empowers your employees with the choice to select an insurance plan that best fits their needs, a feature highly valued in the modern workforce.
**What makes the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction so powerful?**

Its power lies in being an "above-the-line" deduction. This means it directly reduces your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI), which can have a cascading positive effect on other aspects of your tax liability. Unlike a standard medical expense deduction, you get the full benefit whether you itemize or take the standard deduction, and you don't need to meet a high spending threshold to qualify.
Conclusion: From Rule-Taker to Strategist

The intricate rules surrounding S-Corp health benefits are not a barrier; they are a signpost. For the driven professional, understanding this distinction is the pivot from being a passive rule-taker, anxious about compliance, to becoming an active strategist who leverages the tax code with precision. By separating your approach into two distinct frameworks—one for you as the owner and one for your company as the CEO—you transform operational friction into a powerful opportunity.
You now have two clear missions:

items: **As the Owner:** Your mission is personal financial optimization. By executing the prescribed method—S-Corp payment, W-2 reporting, and the Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction—you secure significant tax-free benefits for yourself and your family.

**As the CEO:** Your mission is to build a competitive business. By deploying an HRA for your team, you wield a strategic weapon in the war for talent, offering modern, flexible benefits that rival larger corporations while maintaining absolute budgetary control.

body.content: By embracing these two roles, you eliminate ambiguity. The rules are no longer a source of risk but a clear set of instructions for success. You are in control, positioned to optimize your personal tax situation while simultaneously building a more formidable business.
